content.body: Genetics is the study of heredity ‚ÄĒ how characteristics are passed from parents to offspring. The key molecules carrying genetic information are DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid), organised into chromosomes and genes.

Key definitions:
DNA: double helix molecule made of nucleotides (sugar + phosphate + base). Carries genetic code.
Gene: a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein or trait.
Chromosome: tightly coiled DNA + protein structure. Humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs).
Allele: different versions of the same gene (e.g. tall vs short allele for height).
Locus: the specific position of a gene on a chromosome.

content.title: DNA Structure
content.body:
DNA is a double helix ‚ÄĒ two strands wound around each other.
Base pairing rules (ALWAYS pair the same way):
Adenine (A) pairs with Thymine (T)
Guanine (G) pairs with Cytosine (C)

If one strand reads: A-T-G-C-A
The complementary strand reads: T-A-C-G-T

Genotype vs Phenotype:
Genotype: the genetic make-up (alleles present). e.g. Tt, TT, tt
Phenotype: the physical appearance/characteristic. e.g. tall or short

Dominant allele (T): expressed even if only one copy present
Recessive allele (t): only expressed if TWO copies present

Homozygous dominant: TT (tall)
Heterozygous: Tt (tall ‚ÄĒ dominant expressed)
Homozygous recessive: tt (short)

content.title: Monohybrid Cross ‚ÄĒ Punnett Square
content.body:
Cross two heterozygous tall plants (Tt √ó Tt):

     T    t
T  [ TT ] [ Tt ]
t  [ Tt ] [ tt ]

Results: TT : Tt : tt = 1 : 2 : 1
Phenotype ratio: 3 tall : 1 short
75% tall, 25% short

questions.question: How many chromosomes do human body cells contain?
questions.explanation: Humans have 46 chromosomes in 23 pairs in each body (somatic) cell.

questions.question: What does a gene code for?
questions.explanation: A gene is a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein (or trait).

questions.question: Which bases pair together in DNA?
questions.explanation: Base pairing rules: Adenine pairs with Thymine (A-T), Guanine pairs with Cytosine (G-C).

questions.question: A plant with genotype Tt is:
questions.explanation: Heterozygous means having two different alleles (one dominant, one recessive) for a gene.

questions.question: If T (tall) is dominant and t (short) is recessive, what is the phenotype of Tt?
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed even when only one copy is present. Tt is tall.

questions.question: In a Tt √ó Tt cross, what proportion of offspring are tt?
questions.explanation: From Punnett square: TT, Tt, Tt, tt. One out of four (1/4 = 25%) is tt.

questions.question: What is the phenotype ratio from a Tt √ó Tt cross?
questions.explanation: 3 tall (TT + Tt + Tt) : 1 short (tt) = 3:1 phenotype ratio.

questions.question: Phenotype refers to:
options: The physical characteristics an organism shows
questions.answer: The physical characteristics an organism shows
questions.explanation: Phenotype is the observable physical characteristics (appearance) of an organism.

questions.question: Different versions of the same gene are called:
questions.explanation: Alleles are different forms/versions of the same gene (e.g. tall allele T and short allele t).

questions.question: A cross between TT and tt gives offspring genotype:
questions.explanation: TT √ó tt: every offspring gets T from one parent and t from the other. All offspring are Tt.

content.body: Sex in humans is determined by sex chromosomes. Females have two X chromosomes (XX). Males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY). The 23rd pair of human chromosomes are the sex chromosomes.

Sex Determination Cross:
Mother (XX) √ó Father (XY)

     X    X
X  [ XX ] [ XX ]
Y  [ XY ] [ XY ]

Result: 50% XX (female) : 50% XY (male)
The FATHER determines the sex of the child!

Sex-linked characteristics are controlled by genes carried on the sex chromosomes (usually the X chromosome). Because males have only ONE X chromosome, they only need ONE copy of a recessive allele to show the trait. Females need TWO copies (one on each X).

content.title: Colour Blindness (X-linked)
content.body:
Colour blindness gene is on the X chromosome.
Let XŠīļ = normal vision (dominant)
Let X‚ĀŅ = colour blind (recessive)

Female carrier (XŠīļX‚ĀŅ) √ó Normal male (XŠīļY):

       XŠīļ    X‚ĀŅ
XŠīļ  [XŠīļXŠīļ] [XŠīļX‚ĀŅ]
Y   [XŠīļY]  [X‚ĀŅY]

XŠīļXŠīļ = normal female
XŠīļX‚ĀŅ = carrier female (normal vision)
XŠīļY = normal male
X‚ĀŅY = colour blind male (50% of sons!)

Other X-linked conditions: haemophilia (blood clotting disorder), Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
Why are males more often affected? They cannot be carriers ‚ÄĒ they either have the condition (X‚ĀŅ Y) or not (XŠīļ Y). Females can be carriers (XŠīļX‚ĀŅ) and appear normal.

questions.question: What are the sex chromosomes of a human female?
questions.explanation: Human females have two X chromosomes (XX). Males have XY.

questions.question: Who determines the sex of a child?
questions.explanation: The mother can only give X. The father gives either X (‚Üígirl) or Y (‚Üíboy), so the father's sperm determines sex.

questions.question: What percentage of children from any pregnancy are expected to be male?
questions.explanation: XX √ó XY cross always gives 50% XX (female) and 50% XY (male).

questions.question: Why are males more often colour blind than females?
options: Males have only one X chromosome, so one recessive allele causes the condition
Females can never be colour blind
The gene is on the Y chromosome
questions.answer: Males have only one X chromosome, so one recessive allele causes the condition
questions.explanation: Males (XY) have only one X. One copy of the recessive colour blind allele (X‚ĀŅ) is enough to cause colour blindness. Females need two copies.

questions.question: A woman with genotype XŠīļX‚ĀŅ is called:
questions.explanation: A carrier has the recessive allele but doesn't show the condition (because the dominant allele masks it). She can pass it to her children.

questions.question: X-linked genes are located on:
questions.explanation: X-linked (sex-linked) characteristics are controlled by genes on the X chromosome.

questions.question: A colour blind father (X‚ĀŅY) and normal homozygous mother (XŠīļXŠīļ). What are the sons' genotypes?
questions.explanation: Sons get Y from father and XŠīļ from mother. All sons: XŠīļY = normal vision.

questions.question: From the same cross (X‚ĀŅY father √ó XŠīļXŠīļ mother), what are the daughters?
questions.explanation: Daughters get X‚ĀŅ from father and XŠīļ from mother ‚Üí all daughters are XŠīļX‚ĀŅ (carriers).

questions.question: Haemophilia is caused by:
questions.explanation: Haemophilia is caused by a recessive allele on the X chromosome ‚ÄĒ it is sex-linked.

questions.question: Can a colour blind woman exist?
options: Yes ‚ÄĒ if she has genotype X‚ĀŅX‚ĀŅ
Only if she inherits Y from her father
Only if she has one X chromosome
questions.answer: Yes ‚ÄĒ if she has genotype X‚ĀŅX‚ĀŅ
questions.explanation: A female can be colour blind if she inherits a recessive colour blind allele from BOTH parents (X‚ĀŅX‚ĀŅ), though this is rare.
